# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a Python data processing application. Start from a full Ubuntu base image and install Python, pip, curl, wget, git, vim, and other useful development tools. This gives us flexibility for debugging and running various scripts in production.
# Category: container_security

FROM ubuntu:18.04  # Use full Ubuntu base image with the latest version of LTS (Long Term Support) installed by default on new release images as it is generally stable, efficient and secure for development purpose along side most modern applications requirements that we want to support in a production environment or deployment scenario during debugging/testing phase
RUN apt-get update && \
    DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install -yq --no-install-recommends curl git wget vim  # Install other useful tools and dependencies required by the python application. This ensures our image is as close to a production environment (without unnecessary software/tools) at deployment time
RUN DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install -yq --no-install-recommends python3 python3-pip  # Install Python and pip along with other tools. It's generally recommended for developers not aiming to support old versions or systems without these dependencies in the future
RUN echo "alias pyenv='. /usr/local/bin/pyenv'" >> ~/.bashrc && \
    curl -L https://github.com/pyenv/pyenv-installer/raw/master/bin/pyenv-doctor | bash  # Install latest version of PyEnv (to use Python with pyenv easily) and set it up for current shell session to run python3 commands directly without prefix
RUN mkdir -p ~/.local/share/{python,pip} && chown $NONROOT_USER:$NONROOT_GROUP ~/.local # setup the necessary directories in user's home directory with appropriate permissions.  In a non-root environment where you are running these commands as root will not work properly due to insufficient privileges error
WORKDIR /opt/app   # Set working Directory for Python application inside docker container (this can be defined at different stages of the Dockerfile) - in this case it is done after installing everything but before writing any python code.  This directory structure enables us better organization and ease setup with multiple independent applications
COPY . /opt/app   # Copy all contents from current working directory inside docker image (this includes scripts, requirements files etc.) into the appropriate location within our Docker container at ./dockerfile CMD command in specific case if any default commands need to be executed then provide them otherwise it will use by python entrypoint provided or can set as a script name
RUN pip3 install -r /opt/app/requirements.txt  # Install requirements specified inside requirement files into the Docker image for execution within docker container, this step is done when there are some external dependencies our application might need in production scenario (like pymysql or psycopg2), it's up to us whether we want them globally installed with pip install command and if yes then where
EXPOSE 80   # Expose the port number used by your python app. If you would like this exposed for all traffic, use "0.0.0.0" instead in production environments only expose it during development to make testing easier/more convenient as well with some tools and containers if using Docker Compose or Kubernetes
CMD ["python3", "/opt/app/startup_script.py"]  # Set default command for our Python application (it's defined in startup script). This is the last thing that will be run when docker container starts, it can also set to provide some kind of bootstrapping mechanism or load any desired modules into app context if necessary
